news_summary: Recent scientific breakthroughs highlight promising advances in reversing Alzheimer's, uncovering health benefits of watermelon, understanding the transformative effects of fasting, and clarifying coffee’s impact on blood pressure.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes avancées scientifiques mettent en lumière des progrès prometteurs dans la lutte contre la maladie d'Alzheimer, révèlent les bienfaits de la pastèque pour la santé, permettent de mieux comprendre les effets transformateurs du jeûne et clarifient l'impact du café sur la tension artérielle.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances científicos ponen de relieve progresos prometedores en la reversión del Alzheimer, el descubrimiento de los beneficios para la salud de la sandía, la comprensión de los efectos transformadores del ayuno y la aclaración del impacto del café en la presión arterial.

articles.title: Scientists reverse Alzheimer’s in mice with breakthrough nanotechnology
articles.summary: A new nanotechnology treatment reversed Alzheimer’s symptoms in mice by restoring the brain’s natural cleanup system. The specially engineered nanoparticles helped clear toxic amyloid proteins from the brain and repair the blood-brain barrier, which normally protects and regulates the brain’s environment. In one striking experiment, elderly mice treated with the therapy later behaved like healthy younger mice.
articles.content:
An international team of researchers has reported a striking Alzheimer's breakthrough in mice using specially engineered nanoparticles that do much more than deliver medicine. These microscopic particles act as drugs themselves, helping the brain restore its own natural cleaning system and dramatically reducing toxic protein buildup linked to Alzheimer's disease.

The work was led by scientists from the Institute for Bioengineering of Catalonia (IBEC) and West China Hospital Sichuan University (WCHSU), together with collaborators in the United Kingdom. Their findings were published in Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy.

Instead of focusing directly on damaged neurons, the scientists targeted the blood-brain barrier (BBB), a protective network of cells and blood vessels that controls what enters and leaves the brain. In Alzheimer's disease, this system gradually breaks down, allowing harmful proteins to accumulate and damaging brain function over time.

The researchers designed bioactive nanoparticles called "supramolecular drugs" to help restore this barrier and restart the brain's ability to remove waste.

Repairing the Brain's Cleanup System

The human brain uses enormous amounts of energy. In adults, it consumes around 20% of the body's total energy supply, and in children the figure can reach 60%. To meet those demands, the brain depends on an extremely dense network of blood vessels. Scientists estimate the brain contains roughly one billion capillaries, with nearly every neuron connected to its own blood supply.

Growing evidence suggests these blood vessels play a far larger role in dementia than previously thought. Many researchers now believe vascular damage is not simply a side effect of Alzheimer's disease but may actively drive its progression. Recent studies have also linked blood-brain barrier breakdown to early cognitive decline and increased buildup of toxic proteins.

Under healthy conditions, the blood-brain barrier helps clear waste products from the brain while blocking harmful substances such as toxins and pathogens. One of the most important waste proteins is amyloid-β (Aβ), the sticky material that forms plaques associated with Alzheimer's disease.

In Alzheimer's patients, the brain's waste disposal system begins to fail. As amyloid-β accumulates, neurons become damaged and memory problems worsen.

Alzheimer's Plaques Dropped Within Hours

To test the new therapy, researchers used genetically engineered mice that develop high levels of amyloid-β and progressive cognitive decline similar to Alzheimer's disease in humans.

The animals received only 3 doses of the nanoparticles. The effects appeared quickly.

"Only 1h after the injection we observed a reduction of 50-60% in Aβ amount inside the brain," explains Junyang Chen, first co-author of the study, researcher at the West China Hospital of Sichuan University and PhD student at the University College London (UCL).

The long-term results were even more dramatic. Scientists tracked the animals for months using behavioral and memory tests covering different stages of disease progression.

In one experiment, researchers treated a 12-month-old mouse (equivalent to a 60-year-old human) and evaluated it six months later. By that point, the animal was roughly comparable to a 90-year-old human. Despite its age, the mouse behaved similarly to a healthy animal with no signs of Alzheimer's-related decline.

"The long-term effect comes from restoring the brain's vasculature. We think it works like a cascade: when toxic species such as amyloid-beta (Aβ) accumulate, disease progresses. But once the vasculature is able to function again, it starts clearing Aβ and other harmful molecules, allowing the whole system to recover its balance. What's remarkable is that our nanoparticles act as a drug and seem to activate a feedback mechanism that brings this clearance pathway back to normal levels," said Giuseppe Battaglia, ICREA Research Professor at IBEC, Principal Investigator of the Molecular Bionics Group and leader of the study.

How the Nanoparticles Work

A major focus of the study was a protein called LRP1, which acts like a molecular transport system at the blood-brain barrier. Normally, LRP1 recognizes amyloid-β, binds to it, and moves it out of the brain and into the bloodstream for disposal.

But the process is delicate. If LRP1 binds amyloid-β too strongly, the transport machinery becomes overloaded and breaks down. If the interaction is too weak, waste removal does not occur efficiently enough. Either way, amyloid-β starts piling up in the brain.

The supramolecular nanoparticles were engineered to mimic the natural molecules that interact with LRP1. By doing this, the particles appear to "reset" the transport system, allowing amyloid-β to move out of the brain again.

Researchers say this strategy differs from many traditional Alzheimer's therapies because it focuses on repairing the brain's own infrastructure instead of simply attacking plaques directly.

That idea has gained momentum in recent years. Scientists increasingly view Alzheimer's as both a neurological and vascular disease, with disrupted blood flow and blood-brain barrier damage contributing to the spread of toxic proteins.

A Different Kind of Nanomedicine

Most nanomedicine approaches use nanoparticles as delivery vehicles to carry drugs into the body. In this case, the nanoparticles themselves are the therapy.

The research team created the particles using a bottom-up molecular engineering process that allowed them to precisely control their size and the number of ligands on their surface. This precision helped the particles interact with receptors on cell membranes in highly specific ways.

By influencing how these receptors move and function, the nanoparticles improved amyloid-β clearance and helped restore healthier blood vessel activity in the brain.

Researchers say this approach could eventually complement other Alzheimer's treatments, including anti-amyloid antibody drugs. One of the biggest problems facing current therapies is getting enough medicine across the blood-brain barrier safely and efficiently.

Other experimental technologies are also exploring ways to overcome this challenge, including ultrasound-based delivery systems, "brain shuttle" molecules, and additional nanoparticle platforms designed to cross the barrier more effectively.

What Happens Next

Although the findings are promising, the research remains in the animal-testing stage. Many Alzheimer's therapies that worked in mice have later failed in human clinical trials.

Still, experts say the study highlights an increasingly important area of Alzheimer's research: restoring the health of the brain's blood vessels and waste-removal systems.

"Our study demonstrated remarkable efficacy in achieving rapid Aβ clearance, restoring healthy function in the blood-brain barrier and leading to a striking reversal of Alzheimer's pathology," concludes Lorena Ruiz Perez, researcher at the Molecular Bionics group from the Institute for Bioengineering of Catalonia (IBEC) and Serra Hunter Assistant Professor at the University of Barcelona (UB).

The project involved researchers from the Institute for Bioengineering of Catalunya (IBEC), West China Hospital of Sichuan University, West China Xiamen Hospital of Sichuan University, University College London, the Xiamen Key Laboratory of Psychoradiology and Neuromodulation, the University of Barcelona, the Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences, and the Catalan Institution for Research and Advanced Studies (ICREA).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where nanotechnology reversed Alzheimer's symptoms in mice by restoring the brain's natural cleanup system, showing rapid and long-term positive effects. This breakthrough has broad implications for millions affected by Alzheimer's disease worldwide and offers a promising new therapeutic approach beyond current treatments. The article provides detailed context about the mechanism, experimental results, and potential future impact, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Nanoparticles reverse Alzheimer's symptoms in mice by restoring brain cleanup system
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Alzheimer's disease causes memory loss and brain damage because harmful proteins build up and the brain’s protective barrier weakens. Scientists are searching for new ways to stop or reverse this process.

**What happened** – Researchers from Spain, China, and the UK used tiny specially designed nanoparticles to treat mice with Alzheimer’s-like symptoms. These particles helped repair the brain’s blood-brain barrier and remove toxic amyloid proteins. After just three doses, the mice showed a big drop in harmful proteins within hours and behaved like healthy younger mice months later.

**Impact** – This study is important because it focuses on fixing the brain’s own cleaning system instead of only attacking the harmful proteins. The nanoparticles act as a drug themselves and help the brain clear waste naturally. This method could lead to better treatments that work more quickly and last longer than current options.

**What's next step** – The treatment still needs to be tested in humans to see if it works safely and effectively. Scientists also want to combine this approach with other therapies to improve results. The research highlights the need to protect and restore brain blood vessels in Alzheimer’s disease.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists used special nanoparticles to repair the brain’s cleanup system and reverse Alzheimer’s symptoms in mice, offering a new path for future treatments.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des nanoparticules inversent les symptômes d'Alzheimer chez la souris en restaurant le système de nettoyage du cerveau
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La maladie d'Alzheimer provoque une perte de mémoire et des dommages cérébraux à cause de l'accumulation de protéines toxiques et de la dégradation de la barrière protectrice du cerveau. Les scientifiques cherchent de nouvelles façons d'arrêter ou d'inverser ce processus.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs d'Espagne, de Chine et du Royaume-Uni ont utilisé de minuscules nanoparticules spécialement conçues pour traiter des souris avec des symptômes similaires à Alzheimer. Ces particules ont aidé à réparer la barrière hémato-encéphalique et à éliminer les protéines amyloïdes toxiques. Après seulement trois doses, les protéines nocives ont fortement diminué en quelques heures et les souris ont agi comme des jeunes souris en bonne santé plusieurs mois plus tard.

**Impact** – Cette étude est importante car elle se concentre sur la réparation du système naturel de nettoyage du cerveau plutôt que seulement sur l'attaque des protéines nocives. Les nanoparticules agissent comme un médicament et aident le cerveau à éliminer les déchets naturellement. Cette méthode pourrait conduire à des traitements plus rapides et durables que les options actuelles.

**Prochaine étape** – Le traitement doit encore être testé chez l'humain pour vérifier s'il est sûr et efficace. Les scientifiques veulent aussi combiner cette approche avec d'autres thérapies pour améliorer les résultats. La recherche souligne l'importance de protéger et restaurer les vaisseaux sanguins du cerveau dans la maladie d'Alzheimer.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont utilisé des nanoparticules spéciales pour réparer le système de nettoyage du cerveau et inverser les symptômes d'Alzheimer chez la souris, ouvrant une nouvelle voie pour les traitements futurs.
articles.personality_title_es: Nanopartículas revierten síntomas de Alzheimer en ratones al restaurar el sistema de limpieza cerebral
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La enfermedad de Alzheimer causa pérdida de memoria y daño cerebral debido a la acumulación de proteínas dañinas y al debilitamiento de la barrera protectora del cerebro. Los científicos buscan nuevas formas de detener o revertir este proceso.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de España, China y Reino Unido usaron nanopartículas diseñadas especialmente para tratar ratones con síntomas similares al Alzheimer. Estas partículas ayudaron a reparar la barrera hematoencefálica y eliminar proteínas tóxicas llamadas amiloides. Después de solo tres dosis, la cantidad de proteínas dañinas bajó mucho en pocas horas y los ratones se comportaron como ratones jóvenes y saludables meses después.

**Impacto** – Este estudio es importante porque se enfoca en reparar el sistema natural de limpieza del cerebro en lugar de solo atacar las proteínas dañinas. Las nanopartículas actúan como un medicamento y ayudan al cerebro a eliminar desechos de forma natural. Este método podría llevar a tratamientos más rápidos y duraderos que los actuales.

**Próximo paso** – El tratamiento aún debe probarse en humanos para ver si es seguro y efectivo. Los científicos también quieren combinar este método con otras terapias para mejorar los resultados. La investigación destaca la importancia de proteger y restaurar los vasos sanguíneos del cerebro en la enfermedad de Alzheimer.

**Frase clave** – Científicos usaron nanopartículas especiales para reparar el sistema de limpieza del cerebro y revertir los síntomas del Alzheimer en ratones, abriendo un nuevo camino para futuros tratamientos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a vibrant, glowing network of delicate blood vessels shaped like a protective shield around a stylized mouse brain, with shimmering, gentle nanoparticles resembling tiny, softly glowing spheres weaving through the vessels, symbolizing restoration and cleansing of toxic plaques.

articles.title: Scientists uncover surprising health benefits of watermelon
articles.summary: Studies suggest watermelon could be a hidden powerhouse for better health. Researchers found that people who eat watermelon tend to have higher-quality diets packed with more vitamins, fiber, and antioxidants — while consuming less added sugar and saturated fat. Another study showed watermelon juice may help protect blood vessel function and support heart health.
articles.content:
Watermelon has long been a summer favorite, but research suggests this refreshing fruit may offer benefits far beyond hydration. Studies published in Nutrients found that people who regularly eat watermelon tend to have healthier overall diets and may also support better heart and blood vessel function.

Researchers say watermelon provides an impressive mix of nutrients, antioxidants, and naturally occurring compounds linked to cardiovascular and metabolic health. Its high water content and low calorie count also make it an easy way to add more fruit to your diet.

Watermelon Linked to Better Diet Quality

One study examined data from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) to compare the diets of watermelon eaters and non-consumers across the United States. The analysis found that both children and adults who consumed watermelon generally had higher-quality diets overall.

According to the findings, watermelon consumers took in more dietary fiber, magnesium, potassium, vitamin C, vitamin A, lycopene, and other carotenoids. At the same time, they consumed lower amounts of added sugars and saturated fat. The study was originally published in Nutrients in 2022.

That combination is notable because many Americans still struggle to meet recommended fruit intake goals. The Dietary Guidelines for Americans (DGA) recommend between 1.5 and 2.5 cups of fruit daily, yet most adults and children consume only about half that amount.

Compounds in Watermelon May Support Heart Health

A separate clinical trial from Louisiana State University explored whether watermelon juice could help protect blood vessel function during periods of elevated blood sugar. The randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover study involved 18 healthy young adults who consumed watermelon juice daily for two weeks.

Researchers focused on two naturally occurring watermelon compounds, L-citrulline and L-arginine, which are involved in nitric oxide production. Nitric oxide helps blood vessels relax and expand, an important part of healthy circulation and cardiovascular function.

The study found evidence that watermelon juice supplementation helped maintain vascular function during hyperglycemia and influenced heart rate variability.

"We acknowledge that while the sample size was small (18 healthy young men and women) and more research is needed, this study adds to the current body of evidence supporting regular intake of watermelon for cardio-metabolic health. In addition to L-citrulline and L-arginine, watermelon is a rich source of antioxidants, vitamin C and lycopene -- all of which can help reduce oxidative stress and play a role in heart disease prevention," said Dr. Jack Losso, Ph.D., professor at Louisiana State University's School of Nutrition and Food Sciences.

Follow Up Research Continues to Explore Benefits

Since those studies were published, additional reviews and meta-analyses have continued investigating watermelon's role in vascular and metabolic health. Researchers have reported that watermelon consumption and L-citrulline supplementation may improve measures tied to blood vessel flexibility and circulation, including pulse wave velocity and endothelial function.

Scientists are especially interested in watermelon because it is one of the richest natural food sources of L-citrulline. Recent reviews have highlighted the fruit's potential role in supporting nitric oxide production, healthy blood flow, and cardiovascular function, although experts stress that larger long-term studies are still needed.

Watermelon is also packed with lycopene, a powerful antioxidant associated with reduced oxidative stress and potential heart health benefits. Red watermelon varieties tend to contain especially high levels of lycopene.

A Hydrating, Low Calorie Fruit

Beyond the research buzz, watermelon remains a nutrient-dense fruit that is easy to enjoy year-round. A 2-cup serving contains only 80 calories and provides 25% of the daily value for vitamin C along with 8% of the daily value for vitamin B6. The fruit is also made up of about 92% water, making it a hydrating option during hot weather or after exercise.

Whether served at a summer barbecue, blended into smoothies, or added to salads, watermelon offers a simple way to increase fruit intake while adding vitamins, antioxidants, and hydration to your diet.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on scientific studies revealing that watermelon consumption is linked to better diet quality and cardiovascular health benefits, including improved blood vessel function and antioxidant intake. The findings have broad public health significance given the widespread challenge of meeting fruit intake recommendations and cardiovascular disease prevalence. The article provides detailed context on the research methods, nutrients involved, and potential health impacts, making it a substantive and inspiring health-related breakthrough.
articles.personality_title: Watermelon linked to healthier diets and heart benefits, new studies show
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Watermelon is a popular summer fruit known for its refreshing taste and high water content. Scientists have been studying whether eating watermelon can also improve overall health, especially heart health and diet quality.

**What happened** – Researchers analyzed health data from a large U.S. survey called NHANES and found that people who eat watermelon tend to have better diets. These diets include more vitamins, fiber, and antioxidants, and less added sugar and unhealthy fats. Another study involved 18 young adults drinking watermelon juice daily for two weeks. This study showed watermelon juice may help keep blood vessels working well and support heart function by increasing natural compounds that relax blood vessels.

**Impact** – These findings are important because many people do not eat enough fruit, and heart disease is a common health problem. Watermelon offers a tasty, low-calorie way to add important nutrients like vitamin C, lycopene (an antioxidant), and compounds that help blood flow. The research suggests watermelon could play a role in preventing heart issues and improving diet quality.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to conduct larger and longer studies to confirm these benefits and understand how watermelon affects heart and blood vessel health over time. They are also exploring how watermelon’s special nutrients can help protect the body from damage caused by stress and poor diet.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Eating watermelon regularly may improve diet quality and support heart health by supplying important nutrients and helping blood vessels work better.
articles.personality_title_fr: La pastèque liée à une meilleure alimentation et à des bienfaits pour le cœur, selon de nouvelles études
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La pastèque est un fruit d’été apprécié pour son goût rafraîchissant et sa forte teneur en eau. Des scientifiques étudient si manger de la pastèque peut aussi améliorer la santé, notamment celle du cœur et la qualité de l’alimentation.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont analysé des données d’une grande enquête américaine appelée NHANES et ont constaté que les personnes qui mangent de la pastèque ont généralement une meilleure alimentation. Leur régime contient plus de vitamines, de fibres et d’antioxydants, et moins de sucres ajoutés et de graisses malsaines. Une autre étude a fait boire du jus de pastèque chaque jour pendant deux semaines à 18 jeunes adultes. Cette étude a montré que le jus de pastèque peut aider à garder les vaisseaux sanguins en bonne santé et soutenir le cœur grâce à des composés naturels qui détendent les vaisseaux.

**Impact** – Ces résultats sont importants car beaucoup de personnes ne mangent pas assez de fruits et les maladies cardiaques sont fréquentes. La pastèque est un moyen savoureux et peu calorique d’ajouter des nutriments importants comme la vitamine C, le lycopène (un antioxydant) et des composés qui aident la circulation sanguine. La recherche suggère que la pastèque pourrait aider à prévenir les problèmes cardiaques et améliorer la qualité de l’alimentation.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent réaliser des études plus grandes et plus longues pour confirmer ces bienfaits et comprendre comment la pastèque agit sur le cœur et les vaisseaux sanguins sur le long terme. Ils explorent aussi comment les nutriments spéciaux de la pastèque peuvent protéger le corps des dommages causés par le stress et une mauvaise alimentation.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Manger régulièrement de la pastèque peut améliorer la qualité de l’alimentation et soutenir la santé du cœur en fournissant des nutriments importants et en aidant les vaisseaux sanguins à mieux fonctionner.
articles.personality_title_es: Estudios revelan que la sandía mejora la dieta y beneficia la salud del corazón
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La sandía es una fruta popular en verano, conocida por su sabor refrescante y su alto contenido de agua. Los científicos han estado investigando si comer sandía también puede mejorar la salud, especialmente la salud del corazón y la calidad de la dieta.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores analizaron datos de una gran encuesta de salud en EE. UU. llamada NHANES y descubrieron que las personas que comen sandía suelen tener dietas más saludables. Estas dietas contienen más vitaminas, fibra y antioxidantes, y menos azúcares añadidos y grasas no saludables. Otro estudio con 18 adultos jóvenes que bebieron jugo de sandía diariamente durante dos semanas mostró que el jugo puede ayudar a mantener los vasos sanguíneos funcionando bien y apoyar la salud del corazón al aumentar compuestos naturales que relajan los vasos.

**Impacto** – Estos hallazgos son importantes porque muchas personas no comen suficiente fruta y las enfermedades del corazón son comunes. La sandía ofrece una manera sabrosa y baja en calorías de agregar nutrientes importantes como la vitamina C, el licopeno (un antioxidante) y compuestos que ayudan al flujo sanguíneo. La investigación sugiere que la sandía puede ayudar a prevenir problemas cardíacos y mejorar la calidad de la dieta.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren hacer estudios más grandes y largos para confirmar estos beneficios y entender cómo la sandía afecta la salud del corazón y los vasos sanguíneos a largo plazo. También están explorando cómo los nutrientes especiales de la sandía pueden proteger el cuerpo del daño causado por el estrés y una mala alimentación.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Comer sandía regularmente puede mejorar la calidad de la dieta y apoyar la salud del corazón al aportar nutrientes importantes y ayudar a que los vasos sanguíneos funcionen mejor.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, detailed painting of a ripe red watermelon slice glowing with rich lycopene hues, surrounded by flowing streams of clear water symbolizing hydration, intertwined with stylized heart and blood vessel shapes made of delicate, translucent ribbons to represent cardiovascular health and improved circulation.

articles.title: Scientists reveal how seven days of fasting transforms the human body
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered that the human body undergoes a dramatic internal transformation during extended fasting, with major changes appearing only after about three days without food. In a seven-day water-only fasting study, researchers tracked thousands of proteins in the blood and found widespread shifts affecting organs throughout the body — including the brain. While the body quickly switches from burning glucose to fat, the most intriguing biological changes linked to potential health benefits didn’t emerge until later in the fast.
articles.content:
Going without food for several days does far more than force the body to burn fat. Research published in Nature Metabolism revealed that extended fasting sets off widespread biological changes throughout the body, including shifts linked to the brain, metabolism, and immune system.

Scientists found that many of the most significant effects did not appear right away. Instead, the body seemed to enter a very different biological state after about three days without food.

The findings offer one of the clearest pictures yet of what prolonged fasting does inside the human body at a molecular level. Researchers say the work could eventually help scientists develop treatments that mimic some of fasting's effects without requiring people to stop eating for days.

What Happens to the Body During Prolonged Fasting?

Humans evolved to survive periods of food scarcity. When food is unavailable, the body switches from using glucose from meals to relying on stored fat for energy.

Fasting has been practiced for thousands of years for religious, cultural, and medical reasons. It has also attracted growing attention in recent years because of studies linking fasting and intermittent fasting to weight loss, improved metabolic health, and cellular repair processes.

Still, scientists have had only a limited understanding of exactly how the body responds during longer fasts.

To investigate, researchers from Queen Mary University of London's Precision Healthcare University Research Institute (PHURI) and the Norwegian School of Sports Sciences monitored 12 healthy volunteers during a seven day water-only fast. Blood samples were collected daily before, during, and after fasting.

Using advanced proteomics technology, the team tracked roughly 3,000 proteins circulating in the bloodstream. These proteins can provide clues about what is happening across organs and tissues throughout the body.

The Biggest Changes Started After Day Three

As expected, the body quickly shifted from burning glucose to burning fat within the first two to three days of fasting.

Participants lost an average of 5.7 kilograms (about 12.5 pounds), including both fat and lean tissue. After participants resumed eating for three days, most of the lean tissue loss returned while much of the fat loss remained.

But researchers discovered something more surprising.

Large scale changes in protein activity throughout the body did not begin immediately. Instead, major molecular changes became much more noticeable after around three days without calories.

More than one third of the proteins measured changed significantly during fasting. Some of the strongest shifts involved proteins linked to the extracellular matrix, which helps provide structural support for tissues and organs, including neurons in the brain.

The protein changes were remarkably consistent among volunteers, suggesting the body may follow a highly coordinated response to prolonged fasting.

"For the first time, we're able to see what's happening on a molecular level across the body when we fast," said Claudia Langenberg, Director of Queen Mary's Precision Health University Research Institute (PHURI).

"Fasting, when done safely, is an effective weight loss intervention. Popular diets that incorporate fasting, such as intermittent fasting, claim to have health benefits beyond weight loss. Our results provide evidence for the health benefits of fasting beyond weight loss, but these were only visible after three days of total caloric restriction -- later than we previously thought."

Potential Benefits Beyond Weight Loss

Researchers used genetic data from large human studies to examine how the protein changes observed during fasting might influence long term health.

The results suggested possible links to improvements in several biological pathways associated with disease risk and inflammation. Scientists also identified changes connected to proteins involved in brain support structures.

The findings have fueled interest in whether fasting could someday help researchers develop therapies for conditions involving metabolism, aging, inflammation, or neurological health.

Interest in fasting research has continued growing since the 2024 study was published. More recent reviews have linked intermittent fasting to improved insulin sensitivity, healthier lipid levels, and possible benefits for brain function and cardiovascular health.

Other recent studies on prolonged fasting have also found that the body enters a deep ketogenic state during multi day fasts, dramatically changing how cells use fuel.

Scientists Also Found Possible Risks

While fasting may produce beneficial biological effects, researchers caution that prolonged fasting is not risk free.

A later proteomics study involving prolonged water-only fasting found evidence of increased inflammation, platelet activation, and changes in blood clotting related pathways during extended fasting periods. The researchers said these effects may represent temporary stress responses, but they also highlighted the need for more research into the long term health consequences of extreme fasting.

Experts also warn that prolonged fasting can increase the risk of dehydration, electrolyte imbalances, dizziness, muscle loss, and complications for people with underlying health conditions.

Because of these risks, extended fasting should not be attempted without medical guidance, especially for people with diabetes, eating disorders, cardiovascular disease, or other chronic illnesses.

Why Scientists Are Excited About the Findings

Researchers say one of the most important discoveries was the timing of the body's response.

Many popular fasting approaches focus on shorter fasting windows, but this study suggests some of the body's more dramatic molecular changes may require several days of complete calorie restriction.

"Our findings have provided a basis for some age-old knowledge as to why fasting is used for certain conditions," said Maik Pietzner, Health Data Chair of PHURI and co-lead of the Computational Medicine Group at Berlin Institute of Health at Charité.

"While fasting may be beneficial for treating some conditions, often times, fasting won't be an option to patients suffering from ill health. We hope that these findings can provide information about why fasting is beneficial in certain cases, which can then be used to develop treatments that patients are able to do."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about the biological effects of prolonged fasting, revealing molecular changes with potential health benefits beyond weight loss. The study's findings have broad implications for understanding metabolism, brain health, and inflammation, and could lead to new therapies that mimic fasting's benefits without requiring extended fasting periods. The research is detailed, focused, and relevant to public health and medical science, offering hope for future treatments and improved health outcomes.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover how seven days of fasting changes the body inside
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been curious about what happens inside the human body during long periods without food. While short fasts are common, the effects of fasting for several days were not well understood.

**What happened** – Researchers from Queen Mary University of London and the Norwegian School of Sports Sciences studied 12 volunteers who fasted for seven days, drinking only water. They took daily blood samples and used special technology to track about 3,000 proteins in the blood, which show how different parts of the body are working.

**Impact** – The study found that the body starts using fat for energy within the first few days, but more important changes in proteins affecting organs, including the brain, only appeared after three days. These changes suggest fasting can do more than just help lose weight—it might improve how the brain and immune system work. This is the first clear look at the body’s deep changes during extended fasting.

**What's next step** – Scientists hope this new understanding will help create treatments that copy the benefits of fasting without needing to stop eating for days. They also want to study how these protein changes might protect against diseases and help with aging or brain health. However, they warn that long fasts should only be done with medical advice because of possible risks.

**One-sentence takeaway** – After three days of fasting, the human body undergoes important molecular changes that could explain health benefits beyond weight loss and inspire new medical treatments.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques révèlent comment sept jours de jeûne transforment le corps humain
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques s'intéressent depuis longtemps à ce qui se passe à l'intérieur du corps humain lors de longues périodes sans nourriture. Si le jeûne court est courant, les effets d'un jeûne de plusieurs jours étaient peu connus.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université Queen Mary de Londres et de l'École norvégienne des sciences du sport ont étudié 12 volontaires qui ont jeûné pendant sept jours en ne buvant que de l'eau. Ils ont prélevé des échantillons de sang chaque jour et utilisé une technologie spéciale pour suivre environ 3 000 protéines dans le sang, qui montrent le fonctionnement des différentes parties du corps.

**Impact** – L'étude a révélé que le corps commence à utiliser les graisses comme énergie dès les premiers jours, mais que des changements plus importants dans les protéines affectant les organes, y compris le cerveau, n'apparaissent qu'après trois jours. Ces changements suggèrent que le jeûne peut faire plus que perdre du poids — il pourrait améliorer le fonctionnement du cerveau et du système immunitaire. C'est la première observation claire des changements profonds du corps pendant un jeûne prolongé.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques espèrent que cette nouvelle compréhension aidera à créer des traitements qui reproduisent les bienfaits du jeûne sans avoir à arrêter de manger plusieurs jours. Ils veulent aussi étudier comment ces changements protéiques pourraient protéger contre les maladies et aider au vieillissement ou à la santé du cerveau. Cependant, ils avertissent que les jeûnes longs doivent être faits sous contrôle médical à cause des risques possibles.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Après trois jours de jeûne, le corps humain subit des changements moléculaires importants qui pourraient expliquer des bienfaits pour la santé au-delà de la perte de poids et inspirer de nouveaux traitements médicaux.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos revelan cómo siete días de ayuno transforman el cuerpo humano
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han querido entender qué pasa dentro del cuerpo humano durante largos periodos sin comer. Aunque el ayuno corto es común, no se conocían bien los efectos del ayuno prolongado de varios días.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Queen Mary de Londres y la Escuela Noruega de Ciencias del Deporte estudiaron a 12 voluntarios que ayunaron siete días, solo tomando agua. Tomaron muestras de sangre diariamente y usaron tecnología especial para seguir unas 3,000 proteínas en la sangre, que muestran cómo funcionan diferentes partes del cuerpo.

**Impacto** – El estudio encontró que el cuerpo comienza a usar grasa como energía en los primeros días, pero cambios importantes en proteínas que afectan órganos, incluido el cerebro, solo aparecieron después de tres días. Estos cambios sugieren que el ayuno puede hacer más que ayudar a perder peso; podría mejorar el funcionamiento del cerebro y el sistema inmunológico. Es la primera vez que se observa claramente estos cambios profundos durante un ayuno prolongado.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos esperan que este nuevo conocimiento ayude a crear tratamientos que imiten los beneficios del ayuno sin tener que dejar de comer varios días. También quieren estudiar cómo estos cambios en proteínas podrían proteger contra enfermedades y ayudar en el envejecimiento o la salud cerebral. Sin embargo, advierten que ayunos largos deben hacerse con supervisión médica debido a posibles riesgos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Después de tres días de ayuno, el cuerpo humano sufre cambios moleculares importantes que podrían explicar beneficios para la salud más allá de la pérdida de peso e inspirar nuevos tratamientos médicos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a human silhouette composed of interwoven glowing protein strands and molecular structures, transitioning from vibrant glucose symbols near the head to flowing golden fat cells around the torso, set against a soft abstract background that subtly shifts from day one to day seven, symbolizing the deep biological transformation during prolonged fasting.

articles.title: Scientists reveal the surprising truth about coffee and blood pressure
articles.summary: Coffee may give your blood pressure a temporary jolt, but that doesn’t mean it’s secretly wrecking your heart. Researchers say caffeine can briefly raise blood pressure by stimulating your heart and tightening blood vessels, especially in people who don’t drink coffee regularly. But large studies involving hundreds of thousands of people found no strong evidence that moderate coffee drinking increases the risk of developing hypertension. In fact, coffee also contains natural compounds that may help blood vessels function better.
articles.content:
Coffee has been part of human culture for more than 600 years, and today it remains one of the world's most popular drinks.

People now consume nearly two kilograms of coffee per person each year on average, often with strong opinions about brewing methods, beans, and blends. Genetics can also play a role in how much coffee people enjoy, influencing both caffeine metabolism and the brain's reward system.

Coffee can temporarily raise blood pressure, particularly in people who rarely drink it or already have hypertension. But that does not automatically mean coffee needs to disappear from your routine if you are worried about heart health. For most people, moderation matters more than complete avoidance.

So what exactly does coffee do to blood pressure, and how much is considered safe?

What Is High Blood Pressure?

Blood pressure measures the force of blood pushing against artery walls as the heart pumps. Doctors track it using two numbers:

systolic blood pressure, the higher number, measures pressure when the heart contracts and pumps blood through the body

diastolic blood pressure, the lower number, measures pressure when the heart relaxes between beats.

A healthy blood pressure reading is below 120 millimeters of mercury (mm Hg) systolic and below 80 mm Hg diastolic.

Readings that consistently reach 140/90 or higher are classified as high blood pressure, also known as hypertension.

Hypertension is often called a silent condition because it usually causes no symptoms. Left untreated or poorly managed, it can increase the risk of heart attacks and strokes and worsen existing heart and kidney disease.

Around 31% of adults have hypertension, and about half do not realize they have it. Among people taking medication for high blood pressure, roughly 47% still do not have it under control.

How Caffeine Raises Blood Pressure

Caffeine acts as a muscle stimulant and can increase heart rate in some people. In certain cases, this may contribute to an irregular heartbeat called arrhythmia.

It also prompts the adrenal glands to release adrenaline. That response causes the heart to beat faster and blood vessels to narrow, which can push blood pressure higher.

Caffeine levels in the bloodstream usually peak between 30 minutes and two hours after drinking coffee. Its half-life is 3-6 hours, meaning the amount in the blood falls by roughly half during that period.

Several factors influence how quickly the body processes caffeine. Age matters because children have smaller, less developed livers that metabolize caffeine more slowly. Genetics also play a role, since some people naturally break down caffeine faster than others. Regular coffee drinkers also tend to clear caffeine from the body more efficiently.

Research reviews show caffeine from coffee, cola, energy drinks, and chocolate can raise systolic blood pressure by 3-15 and diastolic blood pressure by 4-13 after consumption.

The effect may be more concerning for people who already have hypertension or existing heart or liver disease. Anyone with these conditions should discuss caffeine intake with their doctor.

Coffee Contains More Than Caffeine

Coffee is made up of hundreds of phytochemicals, compounds that influence flavor, aroma, and potentially health.

Some of these compounds may directly affect blood pressure. Melanoidins, for example, help regulate fluid balance and enzymes involved in blood pressure control.

Another compound called quinic acid has been linked to lower systolic and diastolic blood pressure. Researchers believe it may help blood vessels function more effectively, making it easier for them to handle changes in pressure.

Does Coffee Increase the Risk of Hypertension?

Researchers have closely examined whether coffee actually causes long-term high blood pressure.

One review combined data from 13 studies involving about 315,000 people. During the follow-up period, 64,650 participants developed hypertension. Overall, researchers found no clear link between coffee drinking and a greater risk of developing high blood pressure.

The findings remained consistent regardless of gender, coffee intake, caffeinated versus decaffeinated coffee, smoking habits, or study duration.

Some lower-risk findings appeared in five studies conducted in the United States and seven lower-quality studies, but researchers noted those results should be interpreted carefully.

Another study from Japan followed more than 18,000 adults ages 40-79 for nearly 19 years. About 1,800 participants had very high blood pressure (grade 2-3 hypertension), defined as systolic blood pressure of 160 or higher or diastolic blood pressure of 100 or higher.

In that group, people who drank two or more cups of coffee daily had double the risk of dying from cardiovascular disease, including heart attack and stroke, compared to non-drinkers.

Researchers did not find the same increased risk among people with normal blood pressure or mild (grade 1) hypertension (systolic blood pressure 140-159 or diastolic blood pressure 90-99).

The Bottom Line on Coffee and Blood Pressure

Most people do not need to give up coffee completely. Instead, experts recommend paying attention to overall health, caffeine intake, and blood pressure levels.

Helpful steps include:
articles.reasoning: The article provides reassuring scientific evidence that moderate coffee consumption does not increase the risk of developing hypertension for most people, which can alleviate public health concerns and promote informed dietary choices. It offers detailed context on how caffeine affects blood pressure and highlights beneficial compounds in coffee, contributing to a nuanced understanding of coffee's impact on heart health. This has broad significance given the widespread consumption of coffee and the prevalence of hypertension worldwide.
articles.personality_title: New research clarifies coffee’s effect on blood pressure
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Coffee is one of the most popular drinks worldwide, enjoyed by millions every day. Some people worry that coffee might cause high blood pressure, also called hypertension, which can increase the risk of heart problems. Scientists have studied how caffeine, the main ingredient in coffee, affects blood pressure.

**What happened** – Researchers found that caffeine can temporarily raise blood pressure by making the heart beat faster and narrowing blood vessels. This effect is stronger in people who rarely drink coffee or already have high blood pressure. However, large studies involving hundreds of thousands of people showed no clear evidence that drinking moderate amounts of coffee leads to long-term high blood pressure. Coffee also contains other natural compounds that may help blood vessels work better.

**Impact** – This information helps clear up confusion about coffee and heart health. It shows that for most people, drinking coffee in moderation is safe and does not increase the chance of developing hypertension. For people with normal or mildly high blood pressure, coffee does not seem to raise the risk of serious heart problems. This is important because many people enjoy coffee and worry about its effects.

**What's next step** – Experts suggest that people pay attention to their own health and how they respond to caffeine. Those with high blood pressure or heart conditions should talk to their doctor about coffee intake. More research may focus on how different compounds in coffee affect blood vessels and overall heart health.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Moderate coffee drinking temporarily raises blood pressure but does not cause long-term hypertension for most people.
articles.personality_title_fr: Nouvelle recherche clarifie l'effet du café sur la pression artérielle
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le café est l'une des boissons les plus populaires dans le monde, consommée chaque jour par des millions de personnes. Certains craignent que le café puisse provoquer une hypertension artérielle, ce qui augmente les risques de problèmes cardiaques. Des scientifiques ont étudié comment la caféine, l'ingrédient principal du café, affecte la pression artérielle.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Les chercheurs ont découvert que la caféine peut augmenter temporairement la pression artérielle en accélérant le rythme cardiaque et en rétrécissant les vaisseaux sanguins. Cet effet est plus fort chez les personnes qui boivent rarement du café ou qui ont déjà une pression élevée. Cependant, de grandes études avec des centaines de milliers de participants n'ont pas trouvé de preuve claire que boire du café modérément cause une hypertension à long terme. Le café contient aussi d'autres composés naturels qui aident les vaisseaux sanguins à mieux fonctionner.

**Impact** – Ces informations aident à clarifier la confusion autour du café et de la santé cardiaque. Elles montrent que pour la plupart des gens, boire du café avec modération est sans danger et ne augmente pas le risque d'hypertension. Pour les personnes ayant une pression normale ou légèrement élevée, le café ne semble pas accroître les risques de problèmes cardiaques graves. C’est important car beaucoup de gens aiment le café et s’inquiètent de ses effets.

**Prochaine étape** – Les experts recommandent aux gens de surveiller leur santé et leur réaction à la caféine. Ceux qui ont une hypertension ou des problèmes cardiaques devraient consulter leur médecin au sujet de leur consommation de café. D’autres recherches pourraient étudier comment les différents composés du café influencent les vaisseaux sanguins et la santé du cœur.

**Une phrase à retenir** – Boire du café modérément augmente temporairement la pression artérielle mais ne cause pas d'hypertension à long terme chez la plupart des gens.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva investigación aclara el efecto del café sobre la presión arterial
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El café es una de las bebidas más populares del mundo, consumida diariamente por millones de personas. Algunas personas temen que el café pueda causar presión arterial alta, llamada hipertensión, que aumenta el riesgo de problemas cardíacos. Científicos han estudiado cómo la cafeína, el principal ingrediente del café, afecta la presión arterial.

**Qué pasó** – Los investigadores encontraron que la cafeína puede aumentar temporalmente la presión arterial al hacer que el corazón lata más rápido y estrechar los vasos sanguíneos. Este efecto es más fuerte en personas que rara vez toman café o que ya tienen presión alta. Sin embargo, grandes estudios con cientos de miles de personas no mostraron evidencia clara de que beber cantidades moderadas de café cause hipertensión a largo plazo. El café también contiene otros compuestos naturales que pueden ayudar a que los vasos sanguíneos funcionen mejor.

**Impacto** – Esta información ayuda a aclarar la confusión sobre el café y la salud del corazón. Muestra que para la mayoría de las personas, tomar café con moderación es seguro y no aumenta el riesgo de desarrollar hipertensión. Para quienes tienen presión normal o ligeramente alta, el café no parece aumentar el riesgo de problemas cardíacos graves. Esto es importante porque muchas personas disfrutan el café y se preocupan por sus efectos.

**Próximo paso** – Los expertos sugieren que las personas presten atención a su salud y a cómo reaccionan a la cafeína. Quienes tengan hipertensión o problemas cardíacos deben hablar con su médico sobre el consumo de café. Más investigaciones podrían enfocarse en cómo los diferentes compuestos del café afectan los vasos sanguíneos y la salud del corazón.

**Una frase para recordar** – Beber café con moderación eleva la presión arterial temporalmente, pero no causa hipertensión a largo plazo en la mayoría de las personas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a steaming coffee cup gently balanced on a heart-shaped scale, surrounded by softly glowing, stylized blood pressure gauges with flowing lines connecting them, rendered in natural earth tones and muted reds to symbolize the balance between coffee consumption and heart health.
